annotations.label: Transcendental number theory

anchor.snippet: Transcendental number theory is a branch of number theory that investigates transcendental numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has a `NumberTheory/Transcendental/` directory containing the core predicate and several results, but the field itself is not a single decl.

annotations.label: Transcendental number

anchor.snippet: If no such polynomial exists then the number is called transcendental.

annotations.note: `Transcendental R x` is defined as `¬ IsAlgebraic R x` in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Algebraic.Defs`.

annotations.label: Algebraic independence

anchor.snippet: is called algebraically independent over a field K if there is no non-zero polynomial P in n variables

annotations.note: `AlgebraicIndependent R x` states a family `x` is algebraically independent via injectivity of `MvPolynomial.aeval`.

annotations.label: Euler's assertion on logarithms

anchor.section: Approximation by rational numbers: Liouville to Roth
anchor.snippet: Euler asserted [ 3 ] that the number log a b was not algebraic for rational numbers a and b provided b is not of the form b = a c for some rational c

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement asserting transcendence of logarithms of rationals in non-trivial cases was found.

annotations.label: Liouville's transcendence criterion

anchor.section: Approximation by rational numbers: Liouville to Roth
anchor.snippet: He showed that if α is an algebraic number of degree d ≥ 2 and ε is any number greater than zero

annotations.note: Mathlib's `exists_pos_real_of_irrational_root` produces the Liouville inequality `1 ≤ (b+1)^d * |α − a/(b+1)| * A` underlying the criterion.

annotations.label: Thue–Siegel–Roth theorem

anchor.section: Approximation by rational numbers: Liouville to Roth
anchor.snippet: This result, known as the Thue–Siegel–Roth theorem , is ostensibly the best possible

annotations.note: No Roth-style theorem on Diophantine approximation of algebraic numbers found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lang's conjecture refining Roth

anchor.section: Approximation by rational numbers: Liouville to Roth
anchor.snippet: Serge Lang conjectured an improvement of Roth's result

annotations.note: Lang's conjectured refinement of Roth is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hermite's transcendence of e

anchor.snippet: Charles Hermite used auxiliary functions that approximated the functions

annotations.note: Transcendence of `Real.exp 1` is not proved in Mathlib; only the analytical part of Lindemann–Weierstrass is formalized.

annotations.label: Lindemann's theorem on e^α

anchor.snippet: in order to prove that e α is transcendental for nonzero algebraic numbers α

annotations.note: Transcendence of `exp α` for nonzero algebraic α is not proved; only auxiliary analytic estimates exist.

annotations.label: Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem

anchor.snippet: Karl Weierstrass developed their work yet further and eventually proved the Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem in 1885

annotations.note: Only the analytical part (Jacobson's approximation lemma for `exp`) is formalized; the full Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem statement is absent.

annotations.label: Hilbert's seventh problem

anchor.snippet: asked about the transcendence of numbers of the form a b where a and b are algebraic, a is not zero or one, and b is irrational

annotations.note: Hilbert's seventh problem is not formulated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gelfond–Schneider theorem

anchor.snippet: This result, the Gelfond–Schneider theorem , proved the transcendence of numbers such as e π and the Gelfond–Schneider constant

annotations.note: Grep for `gelfond` finds no Gelfond–Schneider statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Baker's theorem on linear forms in logarithms

anchor.snippet: Baker had proved that if α 1 , ..., α n are algebraic numbers, none of them zero or one, and β 1 , ..., β n are algebraic numbers such that 1, β 1 , ..., β n are linearly independent over the rational numbers

annotations.note: Grep for `baker` returns nothing in this area; Baker's theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cantor's uncountability of transcendentals

anchor.snippet: the algebraic numbers could be put in one-to-one correspondence with the set of natural numbers , and thus that the set of transcendental numbers must be uncountable

annotations.note: `Algebraic.countable` shows the set of algebraic numbers is countable, equivalent to Cantor's conclusion.

annotations.label: Schanuel's conjecture

anchor.snippet: Stephen Schanuel conjectured that the answer is at least n , but no proof is known

annotations.note: Schanuel's conjecture is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Zilber's pseudo-exponential structure

anchor.snippet: Boris Zilber published a paper that used model theoretic techniques to create a structure that behaves very much like the complex numbers

annotations.note: No Zilber pseudo-exponential structure appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transcendence measure

anchor.snippet: Such a bound is called a transcendence measure

annotations.note: Transcendence measure as a function on transcendental numbers is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mahler's classification (S, T, U)

anchor.snippet: Kurt Mahler in 1932 partitioned the transcendental numbers into 3 classes, called S , T , and U

annotations.note: Mahler's S/T/U classification of transcendental numbers is not formalized.

annotations.label: Measure of irrationality

anchor.section: Measure of irrationality of a real number
anchor.snippet: is often called the measure of irrationality of a real number x

annotations.note: `LiouvilleWith p x` formalises approximability with exponent `p`, but the irrationality measure as a supremum/scalar function is not defined.

annotations.label: Liouville number (via measure of irrationality)

anchor.section: Measure of irrationality of a real number
anchor.snippet: A Liouville number is defined to have infinite measure of irrationality

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Liouville` directly and shows it equals being `LiouvilleWith` every exponent, capturing the infinite-irrationality-measure characterization.

annotations.label: Roth's theorem (measure form)

anchor.section: Measure of irrationality of a real number
anchor.snippet: Roth's theorem says that irrational real algebraic numbers have measure of irrationality 1

annotations.note: Roth's theorem is not in Mathlib in any form.

annotations.label: U number of degree n

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: A complex number x in this case is called a U number of degree n

annotations.note: Mahler's U-number classes are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Measure of transcendence

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: is often called the measure of transcendence of x

annotations.note: Mahler's transcendence measure ω(x,n) is not formalized.

annotations.label: S number

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: is finite, and x is called an S number

annotations.note: Mahler S-numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: T number

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: are finite but unbounded, x is called a T number

annotations.note: Mahler T-numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Algebraic iff measure of transcendence zero

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: x is algebraic if and only if

annotations.note: Without the transcendence measure, this characterisation is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LeVeque's U-numbers of every degree

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: William LeVeque in 1953 constructed U numbers of any desired degree

annotations.note: LeVeque's construction is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: U numbers have measure zero

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: The Liouville numbers and hence the U numbers are uncountable sets. They are sets of measure 0

annotations.note: Mathlib shows the set of Liouville numbers (with exponent > 2) has Lebesgue measure zero, the U₁ case; general U-numbers are not defined.

annotations.label: Schmidt's existence of T numbers

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: Wolfgang M. Schmidt in 1968 showed that examples exist

annotations.note: Schmidt's existence theorem for T-numbers is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Almost all complex numbers are S numbers

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: almost all complex numbers are S numbers

annotations.note: This metric statement about S-numbers is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mahler: exp sends algebraics to S numbers

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: Mahler proved that the exponential function sends all non-zero algebraic numbers to S numbers

annotations.note: This Mahler result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Algebraically dependent (two numbers)

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: Two numbers x , y are called algebraically dependent if there is a non-zero polynomial P in two indeterminates with integer coefficients

annotations.note: Mathlib's `AlgebraicIndependent R x` handles families; the dependence of a pair is its negation but no named dyadic-pair definition exists.

annotations.label: Algebraically dependent numbers share Mahler class

anchor.section: Measure of transcendence of a complex number
anchor.snippet: There is a powerful theorem that two complex numbers that are algebraically dependent belong to the same Mahler class

annotations.note: Statement about Mahler classes of algebraically dependent numbers is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: U*-number of degree n

anchor.snippet: is infinite, x is called a U*-number of degree n

annotations.note: Koksma's U*-class is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: S*-number

anchor.snippet: are bounded and do not converge to 0, x is called an S*-number

annotations.note: Koksma's S*-class is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: A*-number

anchor.snippet: A number x is called an A*-number if the

annotations.note: Koksma's A*-class (algebraic numbers in Koksma's classification) is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: T*-number

anchor.snippet: are all finite but unbounded, x is called a T*-number

annotations.note: Koksma's T*-class is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Koksma–Mahler classifications equivalent

anchor.snippet: Koksma's and Mahler's classifications are equivalent in that they divide the transcendental numbers into the same classes

annotations.note: Equivalence of Koksma's and Mahler's classifications is not formalized.

annotations.label: n-th root of λ is a U-number of degree n

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that the n th root of λ (a Liouville number) is a U-number of degree n

annotations.note: LeVeque's n-th-root construction is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uncountable family of U-numbers of degree n

anchor.snippet: This construction can be improved to create an uncountable family of U-numbers of degree n

annotations.note: Improvement to uncountably many U-numbers of degree n is not formalized.

annotations.label: Type of an S number

anchor.snippet: is called the type

annotations.note: The type of an S-number is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sprindzhuk: almost all reals are S numbers of type 1

anchor.snippet: Almost all real numbers are S numbers of type 1, which is minimal for real S numbers

annotations.note: Sprindzhuk's theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.
